citation: Sieljacks P, Knudsen L, Wernbom M, Vissing K. Body position influences arterial occlusion pressure: implications for the standardization of pressure during blood flow restricted exercise. European Journal of Applied Physiology. 2018;118(2):303–312. doi:10.1007/s00421-017-3770-2
abstract: This study investigated the effect of body position on arterial occlusion pressure (AOP) in 24 healthy subjects using Doppler ultrasound with both wide (13 cm) and narrow (6 cm) pneumatic cuffs on the thigh. AOP was measured in supine and seated positions to determine the impact of hydrostatic pressure changes and to identify predictors of AOP across positions. Results showed AOP increased significantly from supine to seated for both cuff widths, with narrow cuffs producing substantially higher and more variable AOP values exceeding 600 mmHg in some subjects. Thigh circumference was the strongest predictor of AOP in all conditions, with diastolic blood pressure explaining additional variance for the wide cuff.
methods: Cross-sectional study with 24 healthy subjects (14 men, 10 women; mean age 32 years). Thigh circumference, skinfold, and blood pressure were measured. AOP was determined via Doppler ultrasound (posterior tibial artery) in randomized supine and seated positions using a wide cuff (Hokanson SC12; 13 cm) and a narrow cuff (Hokanson SC5; 6 cm) placed on contralateral legs. Pressure was increased in 5 mmHg increments up to a system limit of 600 mmHg. Multiple linear regression identified predictors of AOP across body positions.
findings: Supine AOP was 148±19 mmHg (wide) and 348±85 mmHg (narrow); seated AOP increased to 177±20 mmHg (wide, p<0.0001, d=1.5) and 409±101 mmHg (narrow, p<0.01, d=0.7). Four subjects exceeded 600 mmHg with the narrow cuff seated. Supine-to-seated AOP correlation was strong for the wide cuff (R²=0.81) but weaker for the narrow cuff (R²=0.50). Thigh circumference was the primary predictor of AOP for both cuffs; diastolic blood pressure added predictive value with the wide cuff (combined adj. R²=0.655–0.659 for wide cuff models). Mean relative AOP change from supine to seated was approximately 20% for both cuff widths.
limitations: Results apply only to lower body BFR; upper body AOP is likely less influenced by body position due to proximity to the heart. AOP was measured at rest, not during exercise, and exercise-induced blood pressure increases would further elevate true AOP during exercise. Prediction equations are specific to the 12-cm and 5-cm cuffs tested. The standing position was not assessed. Clinical population applicability is unknown.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football staff implementing BFR training should measure AOP in the seated position for lower body exercises rather than relying on supine measurements. Using supine AOP overestimates the relative blood flow restriction applied during seated exercise, particularly with narrow cuffs. Wide cuffs are strongly preferred for athletes with large thigh circumferences — common in football linemen — due to unpredictably extreme pressures narrow cuffs can require. Individual AOP measurement by a trained clinician in the exercise position remains the gold standard for safe BFR prescription.
athleteDev: For football players undergoing BFR-augmented hypertrophy work (e.g., post-practice low-load leg training), cuff pressure should be individualized and measured in the exercise position. Athletes with large thigh circumferences — offensive and defensive linemen, running backs — require higher absolute pressures and face greater risk of inadequate or excessive restriction if standardized pressures are used. Incorporating thigh circumference and diastolic blood pressure into AOP estimation using the wide-cuff prediction equations can help standardize relative restriction when direct Doppler measurement is unavailable.
rtp: BFR training is increasingly used in early post-surgical rehabilitation to preserve muscle mass under low mechanical load. This paper underscores that AOP must be re-measured or corrected for body position as the athlete transitions from supine (early rehab) to seated or standing exercises. Using supine AOP throughout a progressive rehab program will cause relative restriction to diminish as the athlete moves to upright positions, potentially blunting the stimulus. Wide pneumatic cuffs should be standard in clinical RTP protocols to ensure safe, predictable blood flow restriction across all stages of return to play.
tldr: When performing BFR training with lower body exercises in a seated position, do not rely on AOP values measured while the athlete is supine — seated AOP averages roughly 20% higher due to hydrostatic pressure differences, meaning a cuff set to 60% of supine AOP may only represent about 50% of true seated AOP. Wide cuffs are far safer and more predictable than narrow cuffs, which can require dangerously high pressures in athletes with large thigh circumferences. For any lower body BFR protocol, measure AOP in the position the athlete will actually exercise in, and use wide cuffs whenever possible.

citation: Chalmers S, Siegler J, Lovell R, Lynch G, Gregson W, Marshall P, Jay O. Brief in-play cooling breaks reduce thermal strain during football in hot conditions. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport. 2019;22(8):912–917. doi:10.1016/j.jsams.2019.04.009
abstract: This laboratory study examined whether three in-play cooling interventions could attenuate the rise in core (rectal) temperature during simulated soccer/football matches in hot, humid conditions (35°C, 55% RH, 30°C WBGT). Twelve well-trained football players completed four counterbalanced trials: a regular match (REG), a match with two 3-min cooling breaks using chilled water at the 30-min mark of each half (COOLwater), the same with an ice towel added around the neck (COOLtowel), and a match with an extended 20-min half-time (HTextended). All three interventions significantly reduced core temperature rise, exercising heart rate, and session RPE compared to REG, with no significant differences between the three interventions in core temperature effect.
tldr: Brief 3-minute cooling breaks during each half of a football match played in hot, humid conditions (30°C WBGT) meaningfully reduce core body temperature rise (~0.25°C), heart rate (~10–13 bpm), and perceived exertion (~1.3–1.4 RPE units) compared to playing without breaks. Simply stopping play and having players drink chilled water for 3 minutes at the 30-minute mark of each half is as effective as adding an ice towel. An extended half-time of 20 minutes produces similar benefits and may be preferred when mid-half stoppages are not desired. These findings validate FIFA's current policy of sanctioning cooling breaks when WBGT exceeds 32°C, and are directly applicable to American football pre-season and early-season hot-weather practices and games.
methods: Twelve healthy well-trained football players (mean age 24±7 y, VO2max 58±3 mL/kg/min) completed four counterbalanced experimental trials in an environmental chamber set to 35°C, 55% relative humidity, and 30°C WBGT. The treadmill simulation covered 10.8 km over 90 minutes using an intermittent sprint protocol. Continuous monitoring of rectal temperature (self-inserted probe 15 cm past the anal sphincter), heart rate, skin temperature, and thermal sensation (200-mm ASHRAE scale) was performed. Urine specific gravity, body mass change for sweat loss, and session RPE were also collected. One-way and two-way repeated measures ANOVA with post-hoc paired t-tests were used for analysis.
findings: All three cooling interventions significantly reduced rectal temperature change versus REG: COOLwater −0.25°C (p=0.004), COOLtowel −0.28°C (p<0.001), HTextended −0.21°C (p<0.001). No significant differences in core temperature effect between the three interventions. Mean exercising HR was lower in COOLwater (−13 bpm, p<0.001), COOLtowel (−10 bpm, p=0.010), and HTextended (−8 bpm, p=0.011) versus REG; COOLwater also produced lower HR than HTextended (−5 bpm, p=0.016). Session RPE was lower in all three intervention trials versus REG (COOLwater −1.4 au, COOLtowel −1.3 au, HTextended −0.9 au; all p<0.05). Skin temperature and thermal sensation did not differ significantly between any conditions. Whole-body sweat rate was similar across all four trials (~1185–1208 g/h).
limitations: The fixed treadmill protocol does not replicate self-paced field play, where players may increase intensity after breaks, offsetting cooling benefits. No true control trial without fluid during breaks was included, making it impossible to isolate rest from fluid intake as the primary mechanism. No exercise warm-up was performed before each trial, potentially influencing thermoregulatory responses. Findings require field-based validation in uncontrolled match conditions.
practicalImplications: For NFL and college football teams practicing or competing in hot, humid conditions (WBGT ≥30°C), incorporating brief 3-minute structured cooling breaks at approximately the 30-minute mark of each half is supported by this evidence. Providing chilled water during breaks is a low-cost, logistically simple strategy that attenuates thermal strain meaningfully. An extended half-time cooling protocol (20 minutes) with more comprehensive strategies — cold towels, hand or forearm cold water immersion, cooling vests — is an alternative when mid-game breaks are not feasible. Athletic trainers should use WBGT thresholds to trigger cooling break protocols during summer camp and early-season games.
athleteDev: During summer two-a-days and pre-season camps in hot climates, scheduling structured 3-minute cooling breaks with chilled fluid at the midpoint of each practice period can reduce cumulative thermal load and fatigue, potentially allowing players to maintain higher training intensities and reducing heat illness risk. The magnitude of core temperature attenuation (~0.25°C) is comparable to benefits provided by a formal heat acclimatization program, suggesting these breaks can serve as a meaningful and practical heat-management tool within existing practice structures.
rtp: Athletes returning from heat-related illness or those deemed high-risk (e.g., sickle cell trait, prior exertional heat stroke) can benefit from structured in-play cooling protocols that reduce peak core temperature during activity. This research supports mandatory fluid and rest breaks as a safety measure for high-risk athletes during hot-weather practice or competition, providing physiological justification for individualized activity modification plans during the return-to-sport process following heat illness.

citation: Goldschmied N, Ratkovich T, Raphaeli M. Brief report: Exploring the icing the kicker strategy in the NFL. Journal of Applied Sport Psychology. 2024. doi:10.1080/10413200.2024.2437166
abstract: This archival study examined the effectiveness of the 'icing the kicker' strategy across 25 NFL seasons (1999–2024) using a generalized estimating equation (GEE) model applied to 1,290 pressure field goal attempts (within the final 2 minutes, score within 3 points, including overtime). Icing — a timeout called by the opposing coach immediately before a decisive field goal attempt — was found to significantly reduce kicking success (74.1% when iced vs. 79.8% otherwise). A secondary analysis of 106 nullified kicks requiring a re-kick did not find a statistically significant deterioration on the second attempt, failing to support the 'twice-taxed' hypothesis for why icing works.
tldr: Opposing coaches in the NFL should use timeouts to ice the kicker in game-deciding field goal situations: data from 25 seasons (1999–2024) confirm that iced kickers convert at a meaningfully lower rate (74.1%) than non-iced kickers (79.8%), and this effect holds regardless of home/away, indoor/outdoor, or regular season vs. playoffs. Kick distance remains the strongest predictor of success. The psychological mechanism likely involves disruption of pre-performance routines and negative rumination rather than simple physical fatigue. Kicker development programs should simulate icing conditions in practice — including unexpected timeout calls at the moment of pre-kick routine initiation — to build resilience under this specific stressor.
methods: Archival data from 25 NFL seasons (1999–2024) obtained via the nflverse GitHub repository. Pressure kicks were defined as field goal attempts within 2 minutes of the end of regulation when the kicking team trailed by 3 or fewer points (or was tied), plus all overtime attempts (n=1,290 after excluding 42 blocked kicks; 935 games, 128 unique kickers). A generalized estimating equation (GEE) model with binary logistic regression accounted for non-independence of repeated observations per kicker. Predictor variables included timeout condition (iced/none/own team), kick distance, arena (indoor/outdoor), season type (regular/playoff), and location (home/away). A separate chi-square analysis of 106 nullified re-kicks tested the 'twice-taxed' hypothesis.
findings: End-of-game pressure kick success rate was significantly lower than non-pressure situations (77.76% vs. 84.32%; t(24)=6.425, p<0.001; d=1.285, large effect). Non-pressure kicking improved over time (r=0.82, p<0.001) but pressure kicking showed no improvement trend across 25 seasons (r=−0.013, p=0.95). GEE Model 1 showed icing significantly predicted reduced scoring (B=0.162, p=0.021); Model 2 treating icing as dichotomous also showed a significant negative effect (B=−0.274, p=0.049). Kick distance was the only other significant predictor (B=0.102, p<0.001). Home/away, indoor/outdoor, and regular/playoff distinctions were all non-significant. Secondary nullified-kick analysis: 78.3% success on second attempts vs. 84.32% baseline; non-significant (χ²=2.52, p=0.112, small effect).
limitations: Archival design limits causal inference about psychological mechanisms. Blocked kicks were excluded (42 total), attributing failure to the kicker rather than teammates. The nullified-kick subsample was relatively small (n=106), occurred at varying distances, and was drawn from non-critical game situations rather than true pressure contexts, limiting the validity of the 'twice-taxed' test. Self-report and observational data on pre-performance routines were unavailable.
practicalImplications: NFL coaches should preserve and use timeouts to ice the kicker in game-deciding field goal situations — 25 seasons of data confirm iced kickers convert at a meaningfully lower rate (74.1% vs. 79.8%). Special teams coaches should incorporate simulated icing conditions in practice, including unexpected timeout stoppages at the moment of pre-kick routine initiation, to build psychological resilience. Kickers may benefit from quiet eye technique, robust pre-performance routines, and systematic exposure to anxiety and self-focus conditions in training to counter the disruptive effect of icing.
athleteDev: Kicker development programs at the college and NFL level should include systematic exposure to icing conditions — simulating opponent-called timeouts during critical practice moments — to build tolerance and maintain consistent pre-performance routines under pressure. The persistent failure of end-of-game kicking to improve over 25 seasons (unlike non-pressure kicking) indicates a specific, unaddressed performance gap under stress that sport psychology integration can target.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play protocols. However, the broader finding that well-practiced motor skills degrade under unexpected psychological disruption is relevant to athletes returning from injury, who may be particularly vulnerable to performance anxiety disrupting automated movement patterns. Structured mental performance work addressing pre-performance routine robustness under unexpected conditions may benefit athletes in the late-stage RTP process.

citation: Lopez MJ, Bliss T. Bye-Bye, Bye Advantage: Estimating the competitive impact of rest differential in the National Football League. arXiv:2408.10867 [stat.AP]. 2024.
abstract: This paper estimated the competitive impact of rest differential in the NFL using Bayesian state-space models applied to 5,679 regular season games from 2002 to 2023, with outcomes assessed on both point differential and betting market point spread. Three rest categories were modeled: bye week advantage (+6 to +8 days rest difference), mini-bye advantage (Thursday Night Football teams with 9–11 days rest), and Monday Night Football disadvantage. The 2011 Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA), which eliminated practice time during bye weeks, was used as a natural experiment. Pre-2011, the bye week advantage was significant (+2.21 points per game); post-2011, this benefit was substantially mitigated (+0.31 points, non-significant), strongly suggesting extra practice — not rest — drove the historical bye week competitive edge.
tldr: The commonly perceived bye week advantage in the NFL has largely disappeared since the 2011 CBA eliminated practice time during bye weeks. Before 2011, teams coming off a bye won by an extra 2.21 points per game on average — comparable to home field advantage. After 2011, that benefit dropped to a non-significant +0.31 points, even as betting markets increased their bye week valuation to +0.97 points. This strongly implies that extra preparation and film study — not physical rest and recovery — was the primary driver of the historical bye week edge. NFL performance staff should maximize structured preparation activities during bye weeks rather than treating them primarily as passive recovery time.
methods: Bayesian state-space models with time-varying team strength parameters fit using Stan (MCMC, 4 parallel chains, 3,000 iterations, 1,000 burn-in) on 5,679 NFL regular season games (2002–2023). Two primary outcomes modeled: point differential and pre-game point spread (from nflreadR). Four models fit: constant bye effect on point differential (Model 1), pre/post-2011 CBA split bye effect on point differential (Model 2), constant bye effect on point spread (Model 3), and pre/post-2011 split bye effect on point spread (Model 4). Rest categories included MNF disadvantage, mini-bye advantage, and bye week advantage. Models controlled for time-varying team strength and a linearly trending home advantage. Model comparison used ELPD via leave-one-out cross-validation.
findings: Pre-2011, bye week teams had a 99.6% posterior probability of positive point differential impact (+2.21 points, 95% CI: 0.61–3.80), comparable to home field advantage. Post-2011, this dropped to 67.9% probability of positive effect (+0.31 points, 95% CI: −1.01 to 1.64), which is non-significant. There is a 96.6% posterior probability the bye advantage in point differential declined after the 2011 CBA. Betting markets moved opposite to actual outcomes: pre-CBA bye spread adjustment was +0.39 points; post-CBA increased to +0.97 points (95% CI: 0.65–1.28), with 98.8% probability of increase. Mini-bye point differential impact was +0.48 points (95% CI: −0.65 to 1.57; non-significant); MNF disadvantage was +0.14 points in game outcomes (non-significant) but betting markets assigned +0.37 points (99.9% probability of positive effect).
limitations: Study limited to regular season games; postseason bye edges may differ as teams retain full practice privileges. COVID-affected games (2020–2021) were largely excluded due to atypical scheduling. The analysis cannot directly quantify the specific practice activities responsible for the pre-2011 bye advantage. Betting market data are aggregated from multiple sportsbooks and may contain noise. The model assumes team strength varies stochastically across seasons, which may not capture sudden mid-season roster changes.
practicalImplications: For NFL football operations and performance staff, the key implication is that scheduled rest alone does not provide a measurable competitive advantage — preparation quality during that rest period does. The loss of bye week competitive benefit after the 2011 CBA suggests staff should maximize the strategic value of bye weeks through enhanced film study, individualized recovery protocols, and targeted physical development work. Scheduling analysts should note that betting markets continue to overvalue the bye week advantage relative to its actual post-2011 impact.
athleteDev: The finding that extra preparation time — not physical rest — drove the historical bye week advantage has direct implications for NFL athlete development programming. Organizations that maximize structured individual skill development, targeted strength work, and strategic preparation during bye weeks are likely to gain more competitive benefit than those who use the week primarily for passive recovery. For Thursday Night Football games, the negligible mini-bye advantage suggests these scheduling inequities are not a major concern for long-term performance planning.
rtp: The NFL bye week analysis has limited direct RTP relevance, but the finding that 7–14 extra days of rest do not provide a significant physiological performance advantage in healthy professional athletes suggests that return-to-play timelines driven purely by calendar rest may be insufficient. Structured, progressive reloading and sport-specific preparation during rehabilitation — rather than passive rest — appears to be the key factor in restoring competitive readiness, aligning with modern RTP frameworks emphasizing progressive loading and neuromuscular readiness over time-based recovery thresholds.

citation: Silva H, Del Coso J, Pickering C. Caffeine and Sports Performance: The Conflict between Caffeine Intake to Enhance Performance and Avoiding Caffeine to Ensure Sleep Quality. Sports Medicine. 2025;55:1579–1592. doi:10.1007/s40279-025-02245-y
abstract: This narrative review examined the conflict between using caffeine to enhance sports performance and avoiding caffeine to protect sleep quality, with particular relevance for athletes competing in the evening or later. The review synthesized evidence on caffeine's ergogenic mechanisms (primarily adenosine receptor antagonism), its well-established performance benefits across sport types (recommended dose: 3–6 mg/kg, ~60 min pre-exercise), and its documented sleep-disrupting effects — including delayed sleep onset, reduced sleep efficiency, and decreased sleep duration — that can persist for hours post-ingestion given caffeine's 2.5–10 hour half-life. Practical strategies for resolving this conflict are discussed, emphasizing individualized experimentation and monitoring.
tldr: Caffeine (3–6 mg/kg taken ~60 minutes before exercise) reliably improves sport performance across a wide range of activities, but when competition occurs in the evening, the same dose can meaningfully impair post-event sleep quality — and poor sleep undermines recovery, immune function, injury risk, and subsequent performance. The solution is individualized testing: athletes should experiment with caffeine dose, timing, and source (gum, capsule, tablet) in simulated competition conditions while monitoring sleep quality. For evening events, using the minimum effective dose (3 mg/kg) and avoiding caffeine within 5–6 hours of bedtime are practical harm-reduction strategies. Habitual caffeine users should consider periodic abstinence to re-sensitize to its ergogenic effects.
methods: Comprehensive narrative literature review using PubMed, Web of Science, and SPORTDiscus. Search strategy combined key concepts related to caffeine (coffee, energy drinks), sleep (sleep quality, insomnia), and athletes (sport, exercise) using MeSH headings and free-text terms. A three-step approach was used: systematic database search, title/abstract screening with duplicate removal, and backward citation searching. Only studies analyzing the effect of caffeine ingestion on athletes' sleep parameters or sports performance were included; studies on recreational athletes, children, and animals were excluded.
findings: Caffeine at 3–6 mg/kg significantly enhances endurance, team sport, and power performance through adenosine receptor antagonism, with meta-analytic evidence supporting benefits in sprint performance, running distance, and perceived exertion reduction. Caffeine consumed 400 mg (~6 mg/kg) up to 6 hours before bedtime significantly impairs sleep quality compared to placebo. Individual response variability is substantial, influenced by CYP1A2 and ADORA2A genetic polymorphisms, habitual caffeine consumption (tolerance), and anxiety levels. Tolerance to ergogenic effects develops gradually but ergogenic benefits remain detectable with chronic use, strongest on the first day of consumption. Doses above 6 mg/kg provide no additional performance benefit but increase side effect risk including insomnia. A combined 20-min nap plus 5 mg/kg caffeine strategy produced higher performance benefits than either alone as a counter to partial sleep deprivation.
limitations: The review is narrative rather than systematic, introducing potential selection bias. High heterogeneity exists among studies in terms of athlete level, sport type, timing and dose of caffeine, and sleep measurement tools (questionnaires vs. polysomnography). The impact of habitual caffeine use on sleep disturbance in athletes is incompletely understood. Genetic testing for CYP1A2 and ADORA2A polymorphisms is not yet standard practice in sports settings. Most studies measure sleep in controlled lab settings that may not reflect real-world competitive environments.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football teams playing night games — Thursday Night Football, Sunday Night Football, Monday Night Football — should develop individualized caffeine protocols. Staff should identify players most sensitive to caffeine-induced sleep disruption, particularly those competing late at night who have early morning recovery activities. Recommended approach: test caffeine dosing in simulated late-game conditions during pre-season; use the lowest effective dose (3 mg/kg) for evening games; prefer anhydrous sources (capsules, gums) for dose precision; pair caffeine use with post-game sleep hygiene protocols. Habitual caffeine users should consider periodic abstinence cycles during the off-season to restore ergogenic sensitivity.
athleteDev: Caffeine is one of the most evidence-supported legal ergogenic aids available to football players, with documented benefits in strength, power, sprint performance, and cognitive function — all highly relevant to football. Strength and conditioning staff should educate athletes on the performance-enhancing potential of caffeine (3–6 mg/kg, 60 min pre-activity) while simultaneously counseling on sleep hygiene to avoid performance decrements from poor recovery. Given that many football players habitually consume caffeine via pre-workout supplements and energy drinks, periodic abstinence blocks during the off-season may help restore sensitivity and maximize in-season ergogenic effects at lower and safer doses.
rtp: For athletes in rehabilitation, sleep quality is a critical and often overlooked component of recovery and tissue repair. Clinicians managing RTP should assess and counsel athletes on caffeine use, particularly those in early post-surgical phases where sleep is most important for healing. Athletes who habitually use caffeine late in the day to manage fatigue during long rehabilitation periods may be inadvertently compromising recovery sleep quality. Transitioning to morning-only caffeine use and reinforcing sleep hygiene practices during rehabilitation can meaningfully improve the recovery environment and support faster RTP timelines.
